---
title: "Talk Data to Me"
subtitle: "Supporting English Language Learners in Statistics and Data Science"
author:
  - name: <u>Liza Bolton</u>
    affiliations:
      - ref: uoa
  - name: Nathalie Moon
    affiliations:
      - ref: uoft
  - name: Samantha-Jo Caetano
    affiliations:
      - ref: uoft
  - name: J. Sparks
    affiliations:
      - ref: uoguelph
  - name: Quin Xie
    affiliations:
      - ref: uoft
  - name: Ping Huang
    affiliations:
      - ref: uoa
  - name: Christy Sun
    affiliations:
      - ref: uoa
  - name: Jingdi Sun
    affiliations:
      - ref: uoft
affiliations:
  - id: uoa
    name: "Waipapa Taumata Rau University of Auckland"
    city: Auckland
    country: Aotearoa New Zealand
  - id: uoft
    name: "University of Toronto"
    city: Toronto
    country: Canada
  - id: uoguelph
    name: "University of Guelph"
    city: Guelph
    country: Canada
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: default
    css: custom.css
    slide-number: c/t
    hash-type: number
    controls: false
    clickable-controls: false
    navigation-mode: linear
    progress: true
    history: false
    footer: "12th International Conference on Teaching Statistics (ICOTS 12) | Brisbane, Australia"
    include-after-body: header.html
bibliography: references.bib
link-citations: true
---



:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="60%"}
### Warm Pacific greetings

<span class="tooltip">Talofa lava<span class="tooltiptext">Samoan</span></span>, <span class="tooltip">Malo e lelei<span class="tooltiptext">Tongan</span></span>, <span class="tooltip">Kia orana<span class="tooltiptext">Cook Islands Māori</span></span>, <span class="tooltip">Fakaalofa lahi atu<span class="tooltiptext">Niuean</span></span>, <span class="tooltip">Mālo ni<span class="tooltiptext">Tokelauan</span></span>, <span class="tooltip">Ni sa bula<span class="tooltiptext">Fijian</span></span>, <span class="tooltip">Fakatalofa<span class="tooltiptext">Tuvaluan</span></span>, <span class="tooltip">Kia ora<span class="tooltiptext">te reo Māori</span></span>. 
:::

::: {.column width="40%"}
<img src="img/MA_I406706_TePapa_Greetings-from-New-Zealand_full.jpg" alt='A vintage sepia-toned postcard collage titled "Greetings from New Zealand." The central image features a stylized outline map of New Zealand with the text "Greetings from New Zealand" embossed diagonally across it. The collage includes multiple photographic portraits of Māori women in traditional attire, hand-colored floral accents featuring pink roses, white daisies, and purple orchids, an early New Zealand flag variation with a Union Jack base, and a shield emblem labeled "The Southern Cross."' style="width: 90%;" class="presentation-image">

::: {.gallery-plaque}
::: {.artist}
Unknown
:::

::: {.artwork-title}
Greetings from New Zealand
:::

::: {.meta}
1900-1910, New Zealand. Gift of Patricia M. Mitchell, 1989. Te Papa (PS.000812)
:::
:::

:::

::::

---

## Acknowledgement of country

We acknowledge the **Jagera** and **Turrbal** peoples as the Traditional Custodians of **Meanjin** (Brisbane), the lands and waterways where we gather, learn, and share knowledge during ICOTS 12.

We pay our respects to their Elders past, present, and emerging, recognizing their enduring and continuous connection to land, water, and community.

---

## Tangata whenua & land acknowledgement 

The teaching and research that informs this presentation were conducted on the traditional lands of
**Māori** under te Tiriti o Waitangi ([Waipapa Taumata Rau University of Auckland](https://www.auckland.ac.nz/en.html)) and of the
**Anishinaabe**, the **Haudenosaunee** and the **Mississaugas of the Credit** under the Dish With One Spoon
Treaty and, more recently, Treaty 13 between the **Mississaugas** and the British Crown ([University of
Toronto](https://www.utoronto.ca/)). Additional teaching and learning experiences at Southwest University, China, also informed this work.

---



---

## Positionality statement

English is the first language I learned, and it is the one I am most fluent in. I have never had my personal success, safety or access to resources rely on my abilities in another language. Many of you have first-hand experience with learning English as an additional language, that I don’t.

Language is not ‘just’ a skill — it is part of culture, identity and how we know others and make ourselves known in turn.

---

## Interactive Elements & References

You can hover over key terms to see inline extra information or full citations.

* Here is an example of an <span class="tooltip">extra detail popup<span class="tooltiptext">This is supplementary information or a hidden note that appears seamlessly on hover!</span></span> built directly into the slide text.
* We can also reference key literature dynamically, like this foundational text [@wickham2016].

---

##

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="60%"}

### With gratitude

We would like to acknowledge the many **students**, **teaching assistants** and **colleagues** who have contributed to our understanding and the development of our teaching practices for supporting English Language Learners. 

Thank you also to our **ICOTS 12 conference proceedings reviewers**, your thoughtful comments helped us improve this presentation and accompanying paper.

:::

::: {.column width="40%"}
<img src="img/MA_I406706_TePapa_Greetings-from-New-Zealand_full.jpg" alt="" style="width: 90%;" class="presentation-image">

::: {.gallery-plaque}
::: {.artist}

:::

::: {.artwork-title}

:::

::: {.meta}

:::
:::

:::

::::

---

## References

::: {#refs}
:::
